coredata.dc:title: N,S-doped carbon dots as dual-functional modifiers to boost bio-electricity generation of individually-modified bacterial cells
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Dan
Wei, Hui-Fang
Song, Rong-Bin
Fu, Jiaju
Lu, Xuanzhao
Jelinek, Raz
Min, Qianhao
Zhang, Jian-Rong
Zhang, Qichun
Zhu, Jun-Jie
coredata.dc:description: A high-efficiency extracellular electron transfer (EET) of exoelectrogens from the inside of the outer membrane to the external environment is of great importance in the bioelectrochemical energy research. In this regard, developing functional modifiers for individual bacteria, meanwhile integrating efficient light-enhanced energy conversion process possess huge potential to achieve excellent EET. Herein, a practical strategy of modifying individual bacteria by using photo-responsive N, S-doped carbon dots (m-NSCDs) is proposed. This suggested that the m-NSCDs can act as dual-functional modifiers which not only afford enhanced conductivity of bacteria, but also serve as photosensitizers to achieve light-enhanced catalytic process. Specifically, this novel design enables the simultaneous utilization of light- and electrochemical-energy, eventually yielding a superior power output performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Extracellular electron transfer
Light-enhanced energy conversion
Carbon dots
Microbial fuel cell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of IL-NH2 functionalized N–S-doped carbon dots, namely modified NSCDs (m-NSCDs)
Cell culture and formation of functionalized bacterial cells
Construction of bacterial cells/carbon felt bio-anodes
TEM analysis of raw bacteria (RB) and functionalized bacteria (FB)
Construction of CytoC and (CytoC + m-NSCDs) probes on Au electrode
Characterization of m-NSCDs and functionalized bacterial cells
The mechanism of light-enhanced DET process

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21775067 and 21834004 ), the International Cooperation Foundation from Ministry of Science and Technology ( 2016YFE0130100 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2017M621694 ) and the program B for Outstanding PhD candidate of Nanjing University ( 201801B027 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) ATR FT-IR spectra of CDs. (b) Surface charge properties of the FB assembly process. Inset: Photographs of RB and FB. (c) Bright field and (d) Fluorescence field image of CLSM for FB. (e) HR-TEM of FB. Inset: High-magnification HR-TEM image of the FB edge. (f) Growth curves of RB and FB in basal medium.

(a) LSV recorded from FB, RB-cultured bioanodes and the abiotic anode under light and dark. (b) LSV of bioanodes under the chopped light. (c) Turnover CV curves of bio-anodes. (d) The MET- and DET-based turnover current density obtained from (c). All the scan rate: 1 mV s−1.

DPV curves of two probes from −0.3 V to 0.3 V (a) and from 0.3 V to −0.3 V (b), scan rate: 1 mV s−1. Insets: the DPV magnification data after background subtraction. (c) Time courses of the current generation in the multiple operational cycles with an external resistor of 1 KΩ. (d) Polarization discharge curves and power density output curves under light and dark.

head.ce:title: N,S-doped carbon dots as dual-functional modifiers to boost bio-electricity generation of individually-modified bacterial cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing, 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing, 210023, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, Beer Sheva, 84105, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ben Gurion University of the Negev

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, Beer Sheva, 84105, Israel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore, 639798, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore, 639798, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A high-efficiency extracellular electron transfer (EET) of exoelectrogens from the inside of the outer membrane to the external environment is of great importance in the bioelectrochemical energy research. In this regard, developing functional modifiers for individual bacteria, meanwhile integrating efficient light-enhanced energy conversion process possess huge potential to achieve excellent EET. Herein, a practical strategy of modifying individual bacteria by using photo-responsive N, S-doped carbon dots (m-NSCDs) is proposed. This suggested that the m-NSCDs can act as dual-functional modifiers which not only afford enhanced conductivity of bacteria, but also serve as photosensitizers to achieve light-enhanced catalytic process. Specifically, this novel design enables the simultaneous utilization of light- and electrochemical-energy, eventually yielding a superior power output performance.

The bio-electricity generation was boosted by using carbon dots as bacterial modifiers which not only afford enhanced conductivity of bacteria, but also endow significantly light-enhanced bio-catalysis.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial electrochemical systems (MESs) that integrate the advantage of both biological and electrochemical reactors can simultaneously recover contaminated fuels and harvest energy and chemicals [1,2]. Not surprisingly, MESs, such as microbial fuel cell (MFC) for power generation [3,4], microbial electrolysis cell (MEC) for hydrogen generation [5], microbial desalination cell (MDC) for salt removal [6] and bioelectrochemical synthesis (BES) for producing valuable chemicals [7], have attracted considerable attention owing to their charming multifunction and eco-environment [8–11]. All MESs share one common principle in anode chamber, where substrates were oxidized to release bio-electrons catalyzed via electroactive bacteria. During the oxidation process, extracellular electron transfer (EET), which occurs at the interface between bacteria and electrodes, plays an important role to determine MESs performance [12–14]. Highly-efficient EET is essential for the practical application of a diverse array of MESs in environment and energy fields [15–18].

However, till now, the reported EET efficiency is still very low and it has become a bottleneck to further enhance the MESs performance. Thus, developing new methods to address this issue is very urgent. It is widely believed that fabricating novel nanomaterials might be a reasonable solution because this type of nanomaterials can dramatically enhance the anodes surface properties and provide a suitable micro-environment for the natural catalytic process of bacteria [19–21]. Although several substantial progresses about anodes engineering have been made, low total biomass loading of naturally formed biofilms limits further improvement of MESs performance [22]. Subsequently, fabricating electrochemically-active biofilms was further demonstrated to outperform naturally occurring biofilms in EET performance [23]. However, it is inevitable that part of the microbes in the hybrid biofilms cannot be directly associated with conductive substrate electrode, leading to a reduced EET efficiency. In view of this, the strategy to modify individual bacteria with functionalized coating would be a promising solution. Recently, our group developed an in-situ polymerization method to coat polypyrrole on individual bacteria surface for enhanced conductivity, and demonstrated that the EET are superior to that of the raw bacteria [24]. Given the above fact, it is reasonable to anticipate that bacteria modified with multifunctional materials would give better performance in EET. More importantly, besides standalone bio-electrocatalysis, introducing other driving forces such as the inexhaustible solar energy to boost EET and achieve multidimensional energy conversion could be another new research direction, however, such a field remains unexplored.

Since carbon dots (CDs) have been widely applied in the fields of sensing, bioimaging and light emitting devices owning to their biocompatibility, conductivity and stable photo-luminescence properties [25–30], as well as in photocatalytic and photoelectrocatalytic systems [31–33], it is logical to consider CDs as a potential photosensitizer/promotor to boost EET. Herein, we propose to dual-functionalize bacteria by employing chemically modified CDs as modifiers. Our results clearly indicate that the prepared CDs can not only act as the modifier of individual bacteria for enhanced conductivity without affecting viability, but also serve as photosensitizer to further accelerate EET by introducing the sun energy. Thus, the functionalized bacteria exhibit improved bio-current generation and high-power output performance in MFC, eventually achieving multiple energy conversion.

In this research, Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 that was mostly well-studied in MESs was selected as the model bacterium, and N–S-doped CDs (NSCDs) were chosen as the modifiers because doping heteroatoms can effectively modulate their electronic characteristics, surface and local chemical features, leading to enhanced catalytic performance [34,35]. The functionalizing process was shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the carboxylic acid capped NSCDs were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of precursors [36], while the amine-terminated ionic liquid (IL-NH2) was prepared as reported previously [37]. Then, the IL-NH2 modified NSCDs (m-NSCDs), which are expected to carry positive charge, were fabricated based on an amidation reaction. Lastly, the positively charged m-NSCDs were bound to the surface of raw bacteria (RB) to form functionalized bacteria (FB) via electrostatic interaction since the outer membranes of RB carried negative charges.

Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 was purchased from American Type Culture Collection (Manassas, VA, USA). Luria-Bertani (LB) broth was purchased from Nanjing Reagent Company. All the other chemicals were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. The water used throughout the experiments was Millipore water (18.2 MΩ). All media and solutions were sterilized before use.

Photoluminescence (PL) was obtained on an F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-Technologies Corporation, Japan). ATR FT-IR spectra was recorded on Nicolet iS10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific). Zeta potentials were measured at room temperature using ZETASIZER nanoseries (Nano-ZS, Malvern). The TEM was carried out on a JEM-2100 with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV while high-resolution TEM was obtained on a JEM-2800. UV–vis absorption spectra (OD600) were recorded on a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured on a PHI 5000 VersaProbe (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). CLSM studies were performed using a Leica TCS SP8 microscope (Germany). A 500 W xenon lamp coupled with an AM 1.5 global filter was utilized as the irradiation source. In the experiment under illumination, the cooling equipment was used to keep the temperature of systems consistent. All electrochemical measurements were performed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660D, Chenhua, China) with a conventional three-electrode system using a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode in M9 buffer solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of IL-NH2 functionalized N–S-doped carbon dots, namely modified NSCDs (m-NSCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The IL-NH2 (1-(3-aminopropyl)-3-methylimidazolium bromide) was first prepared according to the method previously reported [38]. In the experiment, 3-bromopropylamine hydrobromide (2.200 g, 10 mmol) and 1-methylimidazole (0.790 mL, 10 mmol) were added to 25.0 mL ethanol which was refluxed for 24 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The resulting mixture was re-crystallization with ethanol, and then precipitated in ethyl acetate. The resulting solid was then dried under vacuum at 60 °C overnight. 1H NMR (300 MHz, D2O): = 8.85 ppm (1 H, s), 7.59 ppm (1 H, s), 7.52 ppm (1 H, s), 4.39 ppm (2 H, t, J 7.3), 3.95 ppm (3 H, s), 3.20–3.04 ppm (2 H, m), 2.34 ppm (2 H, dt, J 15.1, 7.5).

Then the pristine carboxylic acid capped N–S-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) were synthesized through the hydrothermal treatment of precursors [39]. In the experiment, 0.3 g citric acid and 0.6 g thiourea were firstly dissolved into 5 mL water to form a clear solution under the constantly stirring. Then the solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. Afterward, the sealed autoclave was heated to 180 °C in an electric oven and kept for 4 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the large size particles and dialyzed against water through a dialysis bag (cut-off molecular weight 500 Da) for 2 days. The suspension of NSCDs was finally freeze-dried for further characterization and applications.

Finally, m-NSCDs was synthesized by an amidation reaction between the carboxylic acid capped NSCDs and the IL-NH2. NSCDs (40 mg) and 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 400 mg) were dissolved in DMF (15 mL) by ultrasonication. Then the IL-NH2 (dissolved in 10 mL DMF) was added into the above solution and then vigorously stirring for 12 h to form m-NSCDs. Subsequently, the m-NSCDs solution was dialyzed against water through a dialysis bag (cut-off molecular weight 3000 Da) for 2 days, and finally freeze-dried for further characterization and applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and formation of functionalized bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 was cultured in 15 mL of LB broth medium at 30 °C overnight on a shaker (150 rpm). The bacterial cells were harvested by centrifugation (6000 rpm, 5 min) for further use.

The functionalized bacterial cells (FB) were fabricated through electrostatic interaction between positively charged m-NSCDs and negatively charged Shewanella oneidensis MR-1. The as-obtained Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 cells were washed with ultrapure water three times and then suspended in 10 mL aqueous solution including different amount of the prepared m-NSCDs. The mixture was placed on a shaker (250 rpm) for 12 h, followed by centrifugation (6000 rpm, 5 min) to remove the unbound m-NSCDs modifiers. Then the as-coated Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 cells were washed with ultrapure water three times and restored in ultrapure water for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of bacterial cells/carbon felt bio-anodes
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate the architecture of the bio-anode, the FB restored in ultrapure water were first concentrated by centrifugation (6000 rpm, 5 min). A piece of carbon felt (1 × 1 × 0.2 cm) was then placed into the tube and soaked with FB, followed by lay-up at 4 °C to allow the firmly attachment of FB on the carbon felt. For comparison, the uncoated raw bacteria/carbon felt electrode was also fabricated following the same procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM analysis of raw bacteria (RB) and functionalized bacteria (FB)
ce:section.ce:para: The FB and RB were suspended in a 2% formaldehyde solution (prepared in 10 mM phosphate buffer) overnight for cell fixation, respectively, and then dehydrated using a series of ethanol solution (25%, 50%, 75%, 95% and 100%) [40]. Finally, copper grids were dipped into the ethanol solution of bacterial cells, and dried under ambient conditions TEM tests.

Cells viability was studied by the LIVE/DEAD BacLight bacterial viability kit. Cells with intact or compromised membrane were differentiated based on differential permeability of fluorescent dye [41]. After the MFCs operation, the FB/carbon felt electrode and RB/carbon felt electrode were removed from anodic chambers. These electrodes were soaked with 10 mL of phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) containing 50 μL of SYTO 9 and 50 μL of propidium iodide dyes, respectively. After incubation in a dark for 20 min, these electrodes were washed with phosphate buffer three times to remove unbound residual dyes from the carbon felt. Then the samples were imaged by CLSM. SYTO 9 signals were detected using an excitation wavelength of 480 nm and an emission wavelength of 500 nm. The fluorescence agent (propidium iodide) was excited at 490 nm and the emission was collected at 635 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of CytoC and (CytoC + m-NSCDs) probes on Au electrode
ce:section.ce:para: For the (CytoC + m-NSCDs) probes, 1 mM Cyto C solution including 2.5 mg mL−1 m-NSCDs was placed on a shaker (250 rpm) for 12 h to fabricate (CytoC + m-NSCDs) probes. The Au electrodes were dipped in 5 mM 3-MPA solution for 2 h, and then rinsed by deionized water repeatedly to remove excess 3-MPA. Then the Au electrodes were brought into contact with 1 mM Cyto C/CytoC + m-NSCDs solutions to allow adsorption of Cyto C to the 3-MPA surface.

A H-shaped dual-chamber MFCs were constructed by connecting two equal rectangular cells of 30 mL in volume. The anode chamber contains 30 mL M9 buffer solution with 5% LB broth and 30 mM lactate as the electron donor. The catholyte was 50 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] in 30 mL phosphate buffer (PBS, pH 7.4) solution. The anodes were FB/carbon felt electrode and RB/carbon felt electrode, while the cathode was carbon paper. The anode and the cathode chambers were separated by a proton-exchange membrane (Nafion 211, DuPont) and connected to an external resistance of 1000 Ω with titanium wire. With the addition of substrate (lactate, 18 mM) to the anodic chamber, the cell voltage measurements across the resistor were recorded. Linear sweep voltammetry was used to obtain the polarization curves in one typical cycle at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. The current density and power output density in electrochemical measurements were normalized to the projected area of the anode with no special instruction. All runs were conducted in three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of m-NSCDs and functionalized bacterial cells
ce:section.ce:para: The NSCDs synthesis was confirmed by detailed characterizations (Supporting Information, Figs. S1–S3). Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR FR-IR) of m-NSCDs exhibits typical IL-NH2 absorption features at 2920 cm−1 and 1156 cm−1, corresponding to symmetric stretching and ring in-plane asymmetric stretching of CH2 in imidazolium ring, respectively (Fig. 1 a) [42]. Moreover, a clear blueshift of the C=O stretching frequency from 1704 cm−1 (-COOH) to 1690 cm−1 (amide) is observed. The results confirm that IL-NH2 has covalently bound to NSCDs surface. Zeta potential measurements show that the NSCDs surface charge changes from −21.5 mV to +16.4 mV after chemical modification (Fig. 1b), also suggesting the success of the surface modification. Subsequently, the as-prepared m-NSCDs were employed as modifiers to change RB into FB via electrostatic attraction. As shown, the Zeta potential of the negatively charged bacteria become more positive from −22.8 mV (RB) to −8.0 mV (FB) and the color of FB appears dimmed compared to that of RB with bright orange color (inset of Fig. 1b), indicating that the RB surface has been modified by m-NSCDs. To directly detect the modification, the confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) analysis of bacteria were performed based on the unique fluorescence property of m-NSCDs. The fluorescence field image of FB displays strong green fluorescence (Fig. 1d), in good accordance with the bright field image (Fig. 1c). But the green fluorescence cannot be detected in the CLSM images of RB and NSCDs-modified bacteria (Fig. S4). The results clearly indicate that the green fluorescence in the CLSM image of FB only comes from the m-NSCDs, suggesting the strategy of modifying individual bacteria via positively charged m-NSCDs is successful. Then, we investigated the morphology of FB by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Fig. 1e). Unlike the smooth surface of RB (Fig. S5), the m-NSCDs modified bacteria possess a rough surface and numerous CDs aggregates are scattered on the surface. Besides, the lattice fringe can be observed on the FB edge from the high-magnification HR-TEM image (inset of Fig. 1e), displaying interplanar distances of 0.286 nm in accordance with that of the solo CDs (Fig. S3). The results further confirm the successful modification of m-NSCDs onto bacteria. To probe the effect of m-NSCDs modifiers on the bacteria viability, the proliferation measurement was performed by cell-culture experiment (Fig. 1f). We found that the growth curves of both RB and FB have no significant difference, which confirms the bacteria viability is not affected after the modification. Additionally, the CLSM analysis for bacteria modified by different amount of m-NSCDs were performed to optimize the modification condition (Fig. S6). The mean fluorescence intensity of a single cell reaches a plateau when the m-NSCDs concentration increases to 2.5 mg mL−2, indicating a suitable modification efficiency. Therefore, the optimized value was selected for the following electrochemical performance testing.

Then, the carbon felt (CF) was utilized to construct bio-anode for probing the functional effect of m-NSCDs modifiers on the biocatalytic behavior of bacteria. As shown in Fig. S7 which displays the current generation from electrochemical cell, the current increases with time, meaning bacterial cells were slowly accumulated on anode surface. Notably, the current generation by FB is higher than that of RB, revealing the enhanced interactions between bacteria and the electrode after the surface modification. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy shows that the charge transfer resistance of the FB-cultured CF (FB/CF) electrode (933 Ω) is smaller than that of RB-cultured CF (RB/CF) electrode (1299 Ω), clearly indicating the m-NSCDs modifiers could endow electrical conductivity on cells surface and helpful to facilitate EET from the bacteria to the electrode (Fig. S8). We further found that the FB could produce a slightly higher level of riboflavin (i.e. the electron shuttle) than that of RB in the bio-current generation process, as indicated in the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. S9). Such an increased riboflavin production could also be helpful to boost EET [43,44].

Additionally, linear sweep voltammogram (LSV) analysis of bioanodes were performed to investigate the effect of light illumination on biocatalytic activity, while the abiotic CF electrode that was directly decorated by m-NSCDs was also measured for comparison. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the m-NSCDs-modified abiotic anode almost produce no oxidation current until the potential was scanned to around +0.6 V, where the water splitting reaction was occurred. While the RB and FB-inoculated bio-anodes present significant oxidation activity across the entire investigated potential range, suggesting the effective bio-catalysis reaction toward electron donor. Notably, under the continuous light illumination, the FB curve shows remarkably enhanced current density compared to other three LSV cases of bio-anodes, demonstrating the excellent light-enhanced ability for the bio-anodic oxidation reaction. To further verify such instant photoresponsive ability, the LSV curves of bio-anodes under chopped illumination were examined. As shown in Fig. 2b, the current density of the FB/CF electrode increases rapidly once illuminated by light, while slows down immediately when light was cut off, exhibiting prompt current response to on-off illumination cycles. In contrast to FB behavior, the RB/CF electrode shows a much smaller current response to on-off illumination cycles, and the current density is always lower than that of FB. The above results clearly indicate that the m-NSCDs modifiers have the dual-functional properties, in which one function displays the enhanced electrical conductivity on cells surface and the other is endowed with considerably light-enhanced bio-catalysis behavior. Moreover, it can be seen that the larger fluctuation of FB current density in Fig. 2b is attributed to the considerably light-enhanced bio-catalysis property of m-NSCDs during on-off illumination cycles.

Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 has two distinct pathways for EET: direct contact-based electron transfer (DET) via outer-membrane c-type cytochromes (CytoC), and shuttle-mediated electron transfer (MET) through flavins [45–47]. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) analyses of the bio-anodes for turnover catalytic current were carried out to reveal EET process. As shown in Fig. 2c which displays the baseline-subtracted CV plots, there is a typical redox peak of flavins starting from ~ −0.45 V, showing a flavin-mediated catalytic current. Moreover, another more abrupt catalytic current arose at ~ −0.30 V was detected in all the four curves of bio-anodes, suggesting the direct involvement of CytoC in EET process [48]. Fig. 2d summarized the current density via the MET and DET process obtained from Fig. 2c and would help to illustrate the mechanism underlying our work [49]. Under dark, both DET- and MET-based catalytic currents for FB were higher than that of RB, indicating the enhanced EET process after the m-NSCDs modification. The increasement can be ascribed to the enhanced conductivity (Fig. S8) and the more produced flavins (Fig. S9). Specially, the DET via CytoC clearly occupies the main part of the entire turn-over current. Notably, once in light illumination, the FB exhibits significantly enhanced DET compared to that under dark, while a negligible change presents for RB. It demonstrates the remarkable light-enhanced effect is attributable to the improved DET process via CytoC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of light-enhanced DET process
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the light-enhanced effect of m-NSCDs on the CytoC activity, the sole CytoC and the m-NSCDs modified CytoC (CytoC + m-NSCDs) were selected as probes, respectively, which were anchored on Au electrodes to detect their electrochemical activity through differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) analysis. Fig. 3 a shows DPV from −0.3 V to 0.3 V for the two probes under light and dark, a clear oxidation peak at around 0.02 V assignable to the CytoC oxidation was detected. Furthermore, the accurate oxidation peak potential can be defined from the DPV magnification data by subtracting background (inset of Fig. 3a). As known, the produced peak current density is actually related to the amount of electrochemical active molecules anchored on Au electrode surface. Importantly, we focus on the difference of peak potential which indicated the reaction kinetics of electrochemical active molecules. It is interesting to find that the oxidation peak potential of the illuminated m-NSCDs-modified CytoC (+0.016 V) was negatively shifted compared to that of other three cases (+0.024 V), suggesting the faster reaction kinetics and the light-enhanced ability of m-NSCDs. Similar result can also be found in DPV data from 0.3 V to −0.3 V, which displays the CytoC reduction process (Fig. 3b). As shown, the positive shift of the CytoC reduction peak from 0.060 V to 0.067 V also indicates the CytoC activity was improved after modifying with m-NSCDs. Therefore, the as-observed light-enhanced effect of m-NSCDs on EET can be illustrated by Fig. S10. Upon light illumination, the m-NSCDs that linked to the bacteria surface can act as photosensitizers, and photo-excited energetic electrons and holes will be generated by absorbing photos. Owning to the high oxidizing property of the photo-excited holes, the electrons in reduced outer-membrane c-type cytochromes anchored on Shewanella oneidensis MR-1 are rapidly transferred to the m-NSCDs and bound with its photo-excited holes. Simultaneously, the photo-excited electrons with lower potential are easier to be transferred to the cathode, thus enhancing the bio-anode oxidation reaction and boosting the whole electron transfer path. The main driving force for such electron transfer enhancement is coming from the high oxidizing property of the photo-excited holes of illuminated carbon dots photosensitizers. Moreover, upon illumination, the anodic oxidation potential of the m-NSCDs modified c-type cytochromes is negatively shifted as demonstrated in Fig. 3a, which lowers the anode potential. Then the E (E = Ecathode-Eanode) in the formula (△G = -nFE) is increased and △G becomes smaller, which makes the whole process of electricity generation more feasible in the view of thermodynamic analysis. Based on the enhanced conductivity of bacteria and the considerably light-enhanced bio-catalysis behavior, the m-NSCDs is demonstrated to be endowed with dual-functional modifiers for boosting EET process.

Given the excellent ability to boost the anodic EET, the m-NSCDs-functionalized bacteria are expected to have advantage for achieving high power output in MFC. To confirm this hypothesis, we constructed a classical double-chamber MFC using FB/CF electrode as bio-anode. A similar MFC with RB/CF anode was also fabricated for comparison. As shown in Fig. 3c, the current density output with multiple operation cycles indicates the FB could generate a maximum current density of ~3.9 A m−2, much higher than ~2.7 A m−2 of RB strain. Such an increasement in electricity generation effectively proves the superiority of FB. Furthermore, both the RB and FB-cultured MFCs can continuously keep electricity generation for at least one month, suggesting that the m-NSCDs modifier has no negative effect on the long-time activity of bacteria. To further confirm this, the two bio-anodes after MFC operation were stained with LIVE/DEAD BacLight bacterial viability kit and subsequently examined by CLSM. It can be seen that viable cells (green fluorescence) cover almost the entire area of the CF substrate with negligible red fluorescence by dead cells, and no pronounced differences can be observed between the FB and RB-cultured bioanodes (Fig. S11), indicating high viability of cells in their biofilms. Moreover, the CLSM analysis of FB after MFC operation was also examined. As shown in Fig. S12, the fluorescence field image of FB still displays strong green fluorescence after MFC operation (Fig. S12a), in good accordance with the bright field image (Fig. S12b). The result indicates that the m-NSCDs modifiers can keep the covalent bonding effect along with the whole process of bio-electricity generation. The polarization curves and the power output curves were utilized to further investigate the capability of bioelectricity generation (Fig. 3d). Notably, the dropping slope of polarization curves obtained from FB is smaller than that from RB both under light and dark, implying a smaller internal resistance is achieved because of the boosted EET after the m-NSCDs modification. The power density output curves show that the FB-based MFC obtains a maximum power density (Pmax) of 1128.1 mW m−2, which is 2.38 times as high as that of the RB strain (473.3 mW m−2) under dark. More importantly, under the light illumination, the Pmax is significantly boosted to 1697.9 mW m−2 for FB-based MFC, which is a 2.6-fold enhancement than that of the raw RB cultured-MFC. As shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information), this value is clearly higher than those of previously reported MFCs using modified CF substrate electrodes as anodes.

In summary, a novel dual-functional bacteria-surface modification has been achieved through electrostatic attraction between positively-charged m-NSCDs and negatively-charged bacteria. The m-NSCDs have been demonstrated to not only afford enhanced conductivity of bacteria, but also endow significantly light-enhanced biocatalysis as efficient photo-sensitizers, thus boosting the EET in bio-anodic oxidation process. As a result, the m-NSCDs modified bacteria electrode exhibits a 2.6-fold enhancement in power output over that of the raw bacteria. The present study provides new insights into the fabrication of high performance of bio-anodes in MESs, and more importantly, opens up an exciting opportunity in solar-enhanced biological energy research.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775067 and 21834004), the International Cooperation Foundation from Ministry of Science and Technology (2016YFE0130100), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M621694) and the program B for Outstanding PhD candidate of Nanjing University (201801B027).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dan Guo is currently pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Jian-Rong Zhang and Prof. Jun-Jie Zhu at the Nanjing University, China. Her research interests include the preparation and characterization of nanomaterials and their applications in biofuel cells.

Hui-Fang Wei received her B.Sc. degree from the Henan Normal University in 2017. She is now working as a master student under the supervision of Prof. Jian-Rong Zhang and Prof. Jun-Jie Zhu in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University. Her research interests focus on microbial fuel cells.

Rong-Bin Song received his BS (2010) and MS (2013) from Nanjing Tech University. After that, he started his PhD study in Nanjing University under the supervision of Prof. Jian-Rong Zhang and Prof. Jun-Jie Zhu, and received his PhD degree in 2017. During this period, he also pursued his research in Nanyang Technology University as a joint PhD student under the supervision of Prof. Qichun Zhang. Currently, he continued his post-doctoral research in Nanjing University. His research expertise involves novel functionalized nanomaterials, bacteria cell-surface coating and their applications in the microbial fuel cells.

Jiaju Fu received his bachelor degree from Nanjing University in 2013. He is now working as a PhD candidate under the supervision of Professor Jun-Jie Zhu. His research interests include electrocatalysis and nanosynthesis.

Xuanzhao Lu received his B.Sc. degree from the Hunan University in 2017. He is currently pursuing his Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Jian-Rong Zhang and Prof. Jun-Jie Zhu in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University. His research interests focus on enzymatic biofuel cells and electrocatalysis.

Raz Jelinek is the Vice President and Dean for Research and Development, and Professor of Chemistry at Ben-Gurion University, Israel. His current research interests are nanotechnology, interface and thin films, sensors, and biological membranes. Special emphasis in his research is on biological and chemical applications of carbon dots.

Qianhao Min received his PhD in Analytical Chemistry from Nanjing University in 2012. After joining the faculty of Nanjing University, he was promoted as associate professor in 2015. His research focuses on the development of nanomaterial-facilitated biological separation and mass spectrometric methodologies, and the construction of nanostructured stimuli-responsive drug delivery systems for biomedical applications.

Jian-Rong Zhang was born in 1963 in Wujiang City of Jiangsu Province. He received BSc (1984), MSc (1987), and Ph.D (1990) from Nanjing University. After obtained his Ph.D, he worked at Nanjing University as a Lecturer in 1990 and a Professor in 2003. His research interests focus on biofuel cell and electroanalysis for biomolecule. He has published more than 150 papers and received a number of national and ministerial awards.

Qichun Zhang is currently an Associate Professor of School of Materials Science and Engineering and an adjunct Associate Professor at the Division of Chemistry and Biological Chemistry, School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences at Nanyang Technological University (NTU, Singapore). He obtained his BS at Nanjing University in China, his MS in physical organic chemistry at the Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Prof. Peiji Wu/Daoben Zhu’s group), his MS in organic chemistry (Prof. Fred Wudl’s group) at the University of California, Los Angeles (USA), and completed his Ph.D. in inorganic chemistry at the University of California Riverside (Prof. Pingyun Feng’s group, USA). Then, he joined Professor Kanatzidis’ group at Northwestern University as a Postdoctoral Fellow.

Jun-Jie Zhu is a Professor of Chemistry at Nanjing University, China. He received his BS (1984), and PhD (1993) degrees in Chemistry from Nanjing University. After this, he started his academic career at Nanjing University in 1993. During 1998–1999, he was a postdoctoral fellow in Bar-Ilan University, Israel. His current research interests are analytical chemistry and materials chemistry, which mainly focus on the study of nanobioanalytical chemistry including bioelectrochemistry, nanoelectrochemistry and the fabrication of biosensors.
